title: Fitting a staircase carpet runner with gripper rods
subtitle: Fix a carpet runner to a timber staircase using stair rods and carpet grippers
excerpt: How to fit a carpet runner down a timber staircase using stair rods, gripper strips at the riser-tread junction, and an underlay, covering measuring the runner, cutting the underlay, fitting the grippers, and pulling the carpet tight between treads.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual (carpet fitting). Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Flooring Industry Training Association (FITA) guidance on stair carpeting.

glossaryTerms.definition: A narrow strip of plywood or hardboard with rows of angled tacks or pins, fixed at the junction of a stair tread and riser. The carpet is pressed onto the pins at each step, which grip the underside of the carpet and hold it in tension. Stair grippers are shorter than room-perimeter gripper: typically 25 to 30 mm wide.

A decorative metal or wooden rod fitted across the tread-riser angle at each step to hold a carpet runner in place. Stair rods are fixed to the stringer or riser with clips or brackets. They are both decorative and functional: the runner tucks under each rod at the tread-riser angle.

A length of carpet narrower than the stair width, leaving a border of exposed timber on each side. Runners are fitted with grippers or stair rods and can be removed for cleaning or replacement without disturbing the stair structure.

content.text: A
carpet runner
is fixed to the staircase using either
stair gripper strips
at each step,
stair rods

, or both together. The gripper method pulls the carpet tightly at each step. The stair rod method holds the runner in the angle between tread and riser and works well with heavier traditional runners on period staircases. Most domestic installations today use grippers as the primary fixing and rods for the visual look.
What you will need: materials

A carpet runner cut to width (typically 600 to 800 mm for a standard domestic stair). Stair gripper strips at a length matching the runner width. Stair underlay strips (rubber or felt, cut to the tread depth at the runner width). Stair rods with brackets if using the rod method. Carpet tacks or staples for holding underlay. If the stair timber is bare and rough, sand and seal the exposed side borders before fitting.
Step 1, measure and calculate the runner length

Measure the run of each step: tread depth plus riser height. Add the two together and multiply by the number of steps. Add 50 mm per step for the carpet to tuck into the gripper at each angle. For a 13-step staircase with 250 mm treads and 200 mm risers, the runner needs to be at least (250 + 200 + 50) x 13 = 6500 mm, plus 200 mm at top and bottom for final tucks. Buy generously: carpet cannot be extended.
Step 2, fit the gripper strips

Fix a stair gripper strip at the back of each tread at the tread-riser angle. The gripper sits with its pins pointing up and toward the riser. Its back edge should be 6 mm from the riser face to allow the carpet to tuck into the pin zone. Pre-drill the tread to avoid splitting, then nail or screw the gripper down through its fixing holes. On a closed-string staircase, fit a second gripper on the riser face, pins pointing away from the step: the carpet wraps over the tread edge and grips on both tread and riser.
Step 3, fit the underlay

Cut stair underlay strips to fit the tread depth only (not over the riser). Press the underlay onto the tread between the grippers at the back and the nosing at the front. Secure with a tack or staple at each end. The underlay should not extend onto the riser face; running underlay over the riser creates a lump under the carpet at the nosing angle.
Step 4, lay the runner from the bottom step

Start at the bottom step. Tuck the runner end under the bottom tread or secure it with a stair rod at the first riser. Working upward one step at a time, lay the runner over the tread and press it firmly onto the gripper pins at the back of each tread using the rubber mallet (placed over a scrap of board to avoid marking the carpet pile). Pull the runner taut before pressing it down; a loose section will work free under foot traffic. At each step, check the side borders are even before moving to the next.
Step 5, fit stair rods (if using)

Fit the stair rod brackets to the stringer or riser at each step before laying the runner. Position the bracket so the rod sits in the angle between tread and riser. Lay the runner over the bracket position and press the rod into its brackets to hold the runner in the angle. The rod should sit snugly and not rattle. If the brackets are loose, re-drill and use a longer screw.
Variations

A runner can be shifted by 50 to 100 mm every year or two. Wear on a stair runner concentrates at the nosing. Shifting the runner moves a fresh section of pile into that position. To allow this, use stair rods rather than grippers, or buy 100 mm extra length and leave a tuck at the bottom.
Care

Vacuum the runner along the pile direction. Check the runner tension annually: if any step has worked loose of its gripper, re-press it with the mallet. Replace the underlay when it compresses flat, typically every 10 to 15 years. Rotate the runner position by one step if the nosing wear becomes noticeable before the pile is worn through.

attrs.symptom: Runner shifts sideways during use and the borders become uneven
attrs.cause: The runner was not centred accurately before pressing down; or the stair rods have slackened in their brackets.
attrs.fix: Lift the runner from the loose steps, recentre, and re-press onto the grippers. If using rods, tighten the bracket screws and check the rod itself is seated in both brackets.

attrs.symptom: Runner bunches at the nosing of a step
attrs.cause: Too much runner was allowed between steps; the surplus is folding at the nosing.
attrs.fix: Pull the runner taut at the next step and re-press onto the grippers. If there is genuinely too much runner, work the surplus down to the bottom step and tuck it under.

attrs.symptom: Gripper pins pulling out of the tread when the carpet is pressed on
attrs.cause: Nails too short for the tread timber; or the tread timber is old and soft.
attrs.fix: Remove the gripper and fix with No. 8 x 25 mm screws through the existing nail holes. Screws grip reliably in old softwood where nails pull out.
